OLSZEWSKI, P. J. RENY, AND R. SIEGEL Our objective is to come as close as we can to settling the equilibrium existence ques-tion in FPAs for a wide class of private-value joint distributions, especially those with atoms. We focus on the “standard” FPA, which resolves ties according to a fair lottery among the tying bidders, but we also consider other tie-breaking rules. Knowing when value-atoms are compatible with equilibrium existence broadens the applicability of FPAs by making simple models with only a few discrete values—even just binary “high” or “low” values—feasible options for researchers. It also permits non-parametric estimation of value-distributions from FPA bidding data without assuming away all forms of value-atoms. And it enables new proof techniques that exploit the presence of value-atoms. 2Finally, it serves a pedagogical purpose through better and more thorough explanations of simple examples. This is demonstrated by the following example, and our discussion of it in Section 4.1, which show that when value-atoms are present, equilibria can fail to exist even in otherwise very simple cases, and that seemingly innocuous changes to the players’ value distributions can have a decisive effect on equilibrium existence. EXAMPLE 1: Two players, 1 and 2, each with quasi-linear utility and independent pri-vate values, bid in a standard FPA. Player 1’s value is known to be 1. If player 2’s value is also known to be 1, then both players bidding 1 is an equilibrium because they are both indifferent between winning and losing the tie. If instead player 2’s value is known to be 2, then both players bidding 1 is no longer an equilibrium be-cause player 2 strictly prefers to win the tie and can profitably deviate by bidding slightly more than 1. In fact, there is no pure strategy equilibrium. But there are mixed strategy equilibria—all with the same outcome—in which player 2 bids 1 and, for any x ∈ [0  1), player 1 bids uniformly on the interval [ x 1]. 3But if player 2’s value is equally likely to be 1 or 2, 4 or is uniform on [1  2], then there is no equilibrium at all, in pure or mixed strategies. 5To understand when value-atoms interfere with equilibrium existence, we consider a general FPA model that permits virtually any amount of correlation among the players’ values and covers a wide class of player utility functions including quasi-linear utilities as well as those with arbitrary risk preferences. Our main finding is that whether an equilibrium exists often turns on the properties of a single statistic of the joint distribution of values. Specifically, consider the distribution of the highest of the players’ values and call the lowest value in its support the minimum high-value (mHV) .6 Technical conditions aside, 7 our first main result, Theorem 3, establishes that the standard FPA possesses an equilibrium whenever the mHV satisfies any one of the following conditions: (a) the mHV is zero, which occurs whenever the players’ values can all be arbitrarily close to zero, (b) there is exactly one player whose value is always at 2 See, for example, the proof of Corollary 3 in Section 5.3, which exploits the connection between reserve prices and value-atoms (as in Kaplan and Zamir (2012)). 3 Note that, in the weak* limit as x converges to 1, both players bid 1, which is not an equilibrium. So the set of FPA-equilibria is not closed in the weak* topology or in any topology under which the sequence of uniform distributions on [ x 1] converges to a mass point on 1 as x converges to 1. 4 This is Example 2 in Maskin and Riley (2000). 5 This follows from Proposition C.1 in Supplemental Appendix Section C (Olszewski, Reny and Siegel (2026)). 6 Recall that the support of a probability distribution is the smallest closed set that has probability 1. 7 We maintain two technical conditions: (i) the Milgrom and Weber (1985) absolute continuity condition and (ii) a positive infimum for the associated Radon–Nikodym derivative. EQUILIBRIUM EXISTENCE IN PRIVATE-VALUE FIRST-PRICE AUCTIONS 195 least the mHV, (c) there are at least two players whose values always exceed the mHV, or (d) no player’s value ever exceeds the mHV. In Example 1, condition (d) is satisfied when both players have value 1 since the mHV is then 1, condition (b) is satisfied when player 1’s value is 1 and player 2’s value is 2 since the mHV is then 2, and none of our conditions are satisfied when player 1’s value is 1 and player 2’s value is equally likely to be 1 or 2, or is uniformly drawn from [1  2]. 8Our second main result, Theorem 4, further highlights the significance of the mHV. This result shows that even if none of our sufficient conditions hold and an equilibrium fails to exist, modifying the standard tie-breaking rule at precisely one bid , the mHV, is enough to restore equilibrium existence. The modification of the tie-breaking rule requires singling out, a priori, a favored player and awarding the good to that player whenever he bids the mHV and ties for the winning bid—with all other ties still broken by a fair lottery. This simple modification to the tie-breaking rule can be implemented without knowing any player’s private information and does not require the players to submit to the auctioneer any extra messages with their bids. While identifying the favored player requires some information about the distribution of values, Theorem 5 shows that much less than full information suffices. We also consider reserve prices and show in Theorem 6 that equilibrium existence con-tinues to turn on the properties of the mHV in much the same way as without a reserve price. Finally, we obtain additional results for settings in which each player has a sym-metric counterpart (Theorem 7) or when the players’ values are drawn independently (Theorem 8). Taken together, our equilibrium existence results for the standard FPA, with or without a reserve price, strictly generalize all the results in the literature that we are aware of. Our analysis also applies to a class of Bertrand price competition models in which each firm’s constant marginal cost is private information. The Bertrand price competi-tion model can be obtained from our FPA model through a simple change of variables because of the flexibility with which we model the players’ utilities. In particular, with the exception of Theorem 8, we do not assume that a player’s utility from winning the good strictly decreases with his bid, even though this is the case in virtually all FPA applica-tions. The advantage of this flexibility is that when players are reinterpreted as firms, our assumptions permit their profits to be non-monotone in the price they charge. To prove our main results, we first extend an important result due to Lebrun (1996), who introduced “efficient” tie-breaking. His Lemma 1 shows that with quasi-linear utili-ties and independent private values, an equilibrium always exists for a modified FPA, let us call it the efficient tie-breaking FPA , in which ties among high bids are broken in favor of the tying player with the highest value, with any remaining ties broken by a fair lottery. Our Theorem 1 strengthens this existence result to allow for correlated values and non quasi-linear and unbounded utilities, while also ensuring that players never bid above their values. The efficient tie-breaking FPA is typically non-implementable because the tie-breaking rule depends on the players’ private information. Nevertheless, it is a useful theoretical device because, unlike the standard FPA, the efficient tie-breaking FPA sat-isfies the hypotheses of Reny’s (1999) equilibrium existence theorem for discontinuous games and therefore always has an equilibrium, even when the players’ values have atoms and are correlated to virtually any degree. 8 But if player 2’s value is uniformly drawn from [1  2] and we add a third player whose (independent) value always exceeds 1, then condition (b) or (c) holds (which of the two depends on the lowest value in the added player’s support), so an equilibrium exists. 196 W. OLSZEWSKI, P. J. RENY, AND R. SIEGEL The remainder of the proof of Theorem 3 establishes that under each one of our four conditions on the mHV, any equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA is either an equilibrium of the standard FPA or—and this is our main innovation—can be modified to become one. As in Lebrun (1996), we show in Theorem 2 that if an equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA is not an equilibrium of the standard FPA, then there is precisely one bid at which a high-bid tie occurs with positive probability, and this bid is the lowest bid, b say, in the support of the high-bid distribution. A standard approach to obtaining equilibrium existence has been to add assumptions that ensure that no such ties exist, in which case every equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA (at least one exists) is an equilibrium of the standard FPA. But that approach turns out to be unduly restrictive. Our main new insight is that as long as the tying bid b is not precisely the mHV, the tie can be removed, thereby yielding an equilibrium of the standard FPA. 9 Thus, one need not add assumptions that rule out ties at the lowest bid in any equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA. It is enough to add weaker assumptions that rule out ties at the lowest bid only when the lowest bid is positive and is equal to the mHV. Our conditions (a)–(d) each do so. Once we establish that it is only ties at the mHV that are problematic, we can show that even if none of our conditions (a)–(d) are satisfied, equilibrium existence can be ensured by modifying the standard tie-breaking rule only at the mHV. This is the main insight behind our second main result, Theorem 4. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 describes the model and main-tained assumptions. Section 3 contains our general existence result for the efficient tie-breaking FPA. Section 4 contains our main results, provides outlines of the proofs, and discusses Example 1 in light of our results. Section 5 considers reserve prices, settings in which each player has a symmetric counterpart, and settings in which the players’ values are drawn independently. Section 6 applies our results to Bertrand price competition. Section 7 discusses the related literature, Section 8 provides some concluding remarks, and Section 9 contains the proofs of our results. The accompanying Supplemental Ap-pendix, Olszewski, Reny and Siegel (2026), has three sections. Section A contains proofs not appearing in Section 9, Section B explores the gap between necessary conditions and our sufficient conditions, and Section C provides a few results on non-existence. 2. MODEL AND MAINTAINED ASSUMPTIONS In this section, we describe the model and provide a list of assumptions that we maintain throughout the paper. We denote real-valued random variables by letters with a tilde above them, and we use the letters themselves, without a tilde, to denote realizations of those random variables. For any random variable ˜x, we let Pr( ˜x < x ) denote the probability that ˜x is strictly less than x, and analogously for Pr( ˜x ≤ x) and Pr( ˜x = x), etc. If Pr( ˜x = x) > 0, then we say that x is an atom of ˜x.There is a single indivisible good and there are n ≥ 2 players. Each player i privately observes the realization of a non-negative random variable ˜︁ vi ∈ [0  ∞). 10 These random 9 When b = 0, the tie is removed by having players not participate when their values are zero, instead of bidding zero. When b > 0, the tie is removed by having all the players who bid b when their value is equal to b

In particular, the support of ˜︁ vican be [0 ∞). EQUILIBRIUM EXISTENCE IN PRIVATE-VALUE FIRST-PRICE AUCTIONS

197 variables may be correlated. The good is then sold in a first-price auction (FPA), in which the players simultaneously each submit a bid, which can be any non-negative real number or the number −1, which represents the choice not to participate. 11 The player submitting the highest non-negative bid receives the good and pays his bid, with any ties among high-bidders broken according to a pre-specified tie-breaking rule. If all the bids are −1, then the seller keeps the good and no payments are made. There is no reserve price. (We consider reserve prices in Section 5.1.) The standard FPA is the FPA in which ties among high-bidders are broken according to a fair lottery. We will focus on the standard FPA, but other tie-breaking rules will prove useful as well and will be introduced as needed. If ˜vi = vi and bi ≥ 0, then player i’s (von Neumann–Morgenstern) utility is ui (vi  b i) if he receives the good and pays bi, and is zero if he does not receive the good (and pays nothing). We assume that ui (vi  v i) = 0 and call vi player i’s value for the good because he is indifferent between receiving the good and paying vi and not receiving the good and paying nothing. We assume that ui (vi  b i) is jointly measurable, continuous in bi for each vi , and, for each bi , continuous in vi at vi = bi and strictly increasing in vi .12 Since ui (vi  v i) = 0, strict monotonicity of u i in vi implies that the sign of ui (vi  b i) coincides with the sign of vi − bi. We further assume that if bki ≥ 0 is any sequence converging to bi as k → ∞ ,then ui (vi  b ki ) converges to ui (vi  b i) uniformly in vi ∈ [0  ∞). 13 These assumptions ac-commodate quasi-linear utility functions, ui (vi  b i) = vi − bi , but also accommodate, for example, players with arbitrary risk attitudes. Because we do not assume that ui (vi  b i) is monotone in bi , we are able to use our model to study equilibrium existence in a Bertrand price competition setting with privately known marginal costs (see Section 6). Following Milgrom and Weber (1985), we assume that the joint distribution, ρ, of ˜︁

RENY, AND R. SIEGEL A player’s strategy is a distributional strategy as introduced by Milgrom and Weber (1985): a distributional strategy for player i is a probability measure, μi, on the product space [0  ∞) × ({ −1} ∪ [0  ∞)) of value-bid pairs ( vi  b i), whose marginal distribution over player i’s value coincides with ρ i, the distribution of ˜︁ vi. For any strategy profile 

μ = (μ1     μ n), we call the induced distribution of the highest bid submitted by the 

n players the high-bid distribution (under μ). We say that a strategy profile μ has a tie at a bid b (or that b is a tie of μ) if and only if there is positive probability that two or more players submit a high bid equal to b. If, in addition, b ≥ 0 (i.e., b̸ = − 1) and at least one of the high-bidder’s utilities from receiving the good and paying b is non-zero, then the tie at b is non-trivial . Otherwise, the tie is 

trivial. In a non-trivial tie, some player’s payoff can be affected by how the tie is resolved. We say that a player i who bids bi ≥ 0 when his value is vi overbids if ui (vi  b i) < 0or, equivalently, if vi < b i . We say that a strategy profile μ exhibits no overbidding if the probability that any player overbids is zero, and we say that μ exhibits full participation if the probability that any player bids −1 (i.e., chooses to not participate) is zero. Finally, a strategy profile is an equilibrium if it is a Nash equilibrium of the FPA game starting from the ex ante stage before the players learn their values. 3. EQUILIBRIUM EXISTENCE WITH EFFICIENT TIE-BREAKING Following Lebrun (1996), we define the efficient tie-breaking FPA as the first-price auc-tion in which any tie among the high-bidders is broken in favor of the tying bidder with the highest utility from winning the tie and paying his bid, with any remaining ties bro-ken by a fair lottery. 17 The following result extends Lebrun’s (1996, Lemma 1) efficient tie-breaking result to settings in which utilities may be unbounded and non quasi-linear, values may be correlated, and the support of the joint distribution of values may not be the product of the supports of its marginals. The result also sharpens Lebrun’s (1996) re-sult by excluding overbidding (see Remark 1 below) and pointing out that only non-trivial ties at value-atoms can impede the existence of an equilibrium in the standard FPA. 18 THEOREM 1: The efficient tie-breaking FPA has a no-overbidding equilibrium with full participation . If such an equilibrium has ties , then each tie occurs at an atom of some player’s value-distribution . If such an equilibrium has no non-trivial ties , then it is an equilibrium of the standard FPA .An immediate corollary of Theorem 1 is the following rather general equilibrium ex-istence result for the standard FPA that, apart from the atomless-values condition, uses only our maintained assumptions. COROLLARY 1: If each player’s value-distribution is atomless , then the standard FPA has a no-overbidding equilibrium with full participation .Because we allow unrestricted value-supports, correlated values, and non quasi-linear utilities, this corollary is not covered by Lebrun (1996, 1999), and it generalizes the results of Maskin and Riley (2000), Jackson, Simon, Swinkels, and Zame (2002), and Jackson and Swinkels (2005) applied to private-value FPAs for a single good. 17 Thus, the player i with the highest value vi might not win a tie, at b say, because there may be a tying player j with value vj < v i and utility u j (vj  b ) > u i (vi  b ). 18 See Example 3 in Jackson, Simon, Swinkels, and Zame (2002) for closely related observations in a setting with quasi-linear utilities and compact value-supports. EQUILIBRIUM EXISTENCE IN PRIVATE-VALUE FIRST-PRICE AUCTIONS 199 R EMARK 1: Corollary 1 does not follow from Lebrun (1996) even if we add his as-sumptions of independent values and quasi-linear utilities, because his existence result for the efficient tie-breaking FPA (his Lemma 1) does not eliminate the possibility of overbidding. For example, suppose there are two players with quasi-linear utilities and independent values, with player 1’s value drawn uniformly from [0  1] and player 2’s value drawn uniformly from [3  4]. Then, Lebrun’s (1996) Lemma 1 does not rule out that the only equilibrium is the one in which player 1 always overbids by bidding uniformly on [1  2] and player 2 always bids 2. But Corollary 1 shows that a no-overbidding equilibrium exists. 4. MAIN RESULTS To state our main results, we need some additional definitions. Say that the joint distribution ρ of the players’ values is regular if its Radon–Nikodym derivative f satisfies inf v∈[0 ∞)n f (v) > 0. Regularity implies that the support of ρ is the product of the supports of the marginals 

ρ i. Consequently, for any player, the support of the other players’ values is independent of the value that he observes. Regularity also implies that if E is any subset of the other players’ value profiles that has positive prior probability, then the posterior probability of 

E after player i observes his value vi is bounded away from zero uniformly in vi ≥ 0. 19 Regularity permits virtually any amount of correlation, positive or negative, between the players’ values, and imposes no restrictions on the location or number of atoms in any player’s value-distribution because it is the marginals ρ i that specify any atoms. Regularity is satisfied, for example, when the players’ values are independent because, in that case, 

ρ is equal to the product of its marginals so we may set f identically equal to 1. 20 Regu-larity is also satisfied when f is positive and continuous and the support of ρ is compact. When each player has finitely many possible values, regularity is satisfied if and only if all combinations of the players’ values have positive probability. For each player i, let v i denote the lowest value in the support of ˜vi, and let v denote the lowest value in the support of the distribution of max{ ˜v1     ˜vn}, the highest of the players’ values. We henceforth refer to v as the minimum high-value (mHV). When ρ is regular, it is straightforward to show that the mHV, v, is equal to max{ v1     v n}. Our next result uses regularity to pin down more precisely when an equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA fails to be an equilibrium of the standard FPA and, in that case, when that equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA can be modified to become an equilibrium of the standard FPA. This possibility of modifying a strategy profile to become an equilibrium of the standard FPA allows us to identify the mHV as being a central determinant for equilibrium existence in the standard FPA. THEOREM 2: Suppose that ρ is regular and that μ∗ is a full-participation , no-overbidding equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA that is not an equilibrium of the standard FPA .

Then μ∗ has precisely one tie . This tie occurs at the lowest bid , b say , in the support of the high-bid distribution , is non-trivial , and 0 ≤ b ≤ v. Moreover , unless 0 < b = v holds , μ∗ can be modified to become a no-overbidding equilibrium of the standard FPA , and this modification exhibits full participation if b > 0. 19 All statements about conditional probability are meant for some version of that conditional probability. 20 But notice that the regularity of ρ does not imply bounded supports because regularity allows the supports of the ρi ’s to be unbounded. For example, the supports are unbounded if players’ values are independently distributed according to an exponential distribution, yet f is identically 1, so regularity is satisfied. 200 W. OLSZEWSKI, P. J. RENY, AND R. SIEGEL Theorem 2 shows that under regularity, any equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA either is an equilibrium of the standard FPA or has a non-trivial tie at the lowest bid, 

b, in the support of the high-bid distribution. 21 And in the latter case, the equilibrium can be modified to become an equilibrium of the standard FPA as long as 0 < b = v does not hold. Thus, a non-trivial tie at the mHV is the only possible impediment to equilibrium existence in the standard FPA, which explains why the mHV is so central. The proof of Theorem 2 can be sketched as follows. Observe first that, regardless of the tie-breaking rule, in any non-trivial tie of an equilibrium, there cannot be two tying players whose values exceed their bids, since at least one of them could profitably deviate by bidding slightly higher. So, by no overbidding, all the tying players but one bid their value and the remaining tying player bids less than his value. Moreover, this remaining player is always the same player. Otherwise, if this remaining player were sometimes one player and sometimes another, regularity would imply that they both sometimes tie for the high bid while bidding less than their values, which we have already argued cannot occur. Using now the notation from the theorem, if μ∗ is not an equilibrium of the standard FPA, then it must have at least one tie (otherwise μ∗ would be an equilibrium for any tie-breaking rule). To see why that tie must be at b, suppose that it is at b > b (b < b is ruled out by the definition of b). Then, no tying player’s value is b because, if it were, their bid of b would give them a payoff of zero, but bidding slightly less would yield a positive payoff because the highest bid of the others is below b with positive probability (since b > b and by regularity). But if no tying player is bidding their value, they must all be bidding less than their value (no overbidding), contradicting the previous paragraph. Hence, the unique tie is at b. Moreover, the tie must be non-trivial; otherwise, μ∗ would be an equilibrium of the standard FPA. Hence, by the previous paragraph, there is exactly one player, i say, who ever bids b when his value exceeds b. All other players who bid b

do so only when their values are b, so they are indifferent between winning and losing the tie. By efficient tie-breaking, player i always wins the tie at b when his value exceeds his tying bid of b.If b = 0, things are simple. We can then modify μ∗ so that all the indifferent tying players choose not to participate instead of bidding b, which eliminates the tie without changing the players’ payoffs and results in an equilibrium of the standard FPA. Much less straightforward is the case in which b > 0. The same modification no longer works because it would incentivize player i to bid zero instead of b, and then the other players would want to participate. Instead, we modify μ∗ so that the indifferent tying players bid randomly according to a carefully chosen distribution on a small interval below b

instead of bidding b, once again eliminating the tie without changing the players’ payoffs. The chosen distribution must maintain player i’s incentive to bid b whenever he did so before. Our proof shows that this modification works when 0 < b < v , because (as we show) player i’s value is at least v, so the prospect of reducing the probability of obtaining substantial surplus makes it unattractive for player i to bid less than b. But when b = v,this modification may not work because bidding b gives player i almost no surplus when his value is near v = b, so he may have an incentive to bid less than b (since all the other players now sometimes do so). This, in turn, can incentivize the originally indifferent tying players to bid more than is specified by their modified bids. This is why the theorem 21 Lebrun’s (1996) related Lemma 3 also states that b is the only possible non-trivial tie. However, because Lebrun’s result does not rule out overbidding, he is unable to conclude that b ≤ v.EQUILIBRIUM EXISTENCE IN PRIVATE-VALUE FIRST-PRICE AUCTIONS 201 identifies 0 < b = v as the one exceptional case in which the standard FPA may not have an equilibrium. By Theorem 1, the efficient tie-breaking FPA has an equilibrium. So if we can find conditions on the primitives of our model under which every equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA is either an equilibrium of the standard FPA or is such that 0 < b = v

does not hold, then, by Theorem 2, we will have obtained an equilibrium existence result for the standard FPA. This is precisely what we do in our main result, which we now state. Note the centrality of the mHV. THEOREM 3: Suppose that ρ is regular . Then the standard FPA has a no-overbidding equi-librium if the mHV , v, satisfies any of the following conditions :(a) v = 0. (b) Pr( ˜vi ≥ v) = 1 for exactly one player i.(c) Pr( ˜vi > v ) = 1 for at least two players i.(d) Pr( ˜vi ≤ v) = 1 for every player i.

If , in addition to any of the conditions (a)–(d), at least two players’ value-distributions have no atom at zero , then the standard FPA has a no-overbidding equilibrium with full participation .Condition (a) in Theorem 3 says that the highest of the players’ values can be arbitrarily close to zero. 22 Condition (b) says that there is precisely one player whose value is never lower than the mHV. Neither (a) nor (b) places any restrictions on the number or location of atoms in the players’ value-distributions. Condition (c) says that there are at least two players whose values always exceed the mHV. Condition (d) says that no player’s value exceeds the mHV, which can only happen if at least one player’s value is always equal to the mHV. Notice that because ρ is regular, v = max{ v1     v n} so v i = v for at least one player i.Therefore, in terms of the players’ minimum values, v i, condition (a) says that v1 = · · · = 

v n = 0; condition (b) says that v i = v for precisely one player i; and condition (c) says that 

v i = v for more than one player i and at least two such players have no value-atom at v.The Supplemental Appendix (Olszewski, Reny and Siegel (2026)) explores the scope for an existence result when none of the conditions (a), (b), (c), or (d) hold. R EMARK 2: Theorem 3 generalizes Lebrun’s (1996) existence result with mandatory participation. This is because under Lebrun’s (1996) conditions, Theorem 3 ensures the existence of a full-participation no-overbidding equilibrium of the standard FPA, and any such equilibrium remains an equilibrium if participation becomes mandatory. If each player has only finitely many possible values and all value combinations have positive probability, then the joint distribution of values is regular. If, in addition, zero is a possible value for each player, then condition (a) of Theorem 3 holds. Hence, Theorem 3 immediately yields the following result. 23 22 Maskin and Riley (2000, footnote 6 (i)), referencing an earlier working paper, appear to have been aware that condition (a) can ensure equilibrium existence. But such a result is not formally stated in Maskin and Riley (2000), and is in fact not true under the assumptions made there. For example, if two players with quasi-linear utilities have private values ( ˜v1 ˜v2 ) that are equally likely to be (0  0), (1  1), or (1  2), then v = 0 but the standard FPA has no equilibrium (as in Example 1 when values are equally likely to be (1  1) or (1  2)). Note that regularity fails here because the support of ρ is not a product set. 23 The previous footnote shows what can go wrong if not all combinations of the players’ values have positive probability. 202 W. OLSZEWSKI, P. J. RENY, AND R. SIEGEL COROLLARY 2—Finite values: If each player’s set of possible values is finite and includes zero , and if all possible combinations of the players’ values have positive probability , then the standard FPA has a no-overbidding equilibrium .To state our second main result, which further highlights the centrality of the mHV, we say that a tie-breaking rule is ( i b )-favoring if whenever the highest bid is b ≥ 0 and player i and at least one other player bid b, the tie is broken in favor of player i, who we will call the favored player. This leaves unspecified how other ties are broken. But this does not matter because we are interested in the standard (i b )-favoring FPA, in which the favored player i wins whenever he bids b and it is the highest bid, no matter how many other players also bid b, and all other ties are broken by a fair lottery. Our second main result states that equilibrium existence holds very generally provided that the standard tie-breaking rule can be modified at just one bid, the mHV, and in favor of a single player. THEOREM 4: Suppose that ρ is regular . Then there is some player i such that the standard 

(i v )-favoring FPA has a no-overbidding equilibrium .The proof of Theorem 4 proceeds as follows. If the standard FPA has a no-overbidding equilibrium, then we are done, because this equilibrium is an equilibrium of every FPA, regardless of the tie-breaking rule, including the standard ( i v )-favoring FPA for every player i. If the standard FPA does not have a no-overbidding equilibrium, then, by The-orems 1 and 2, there is a no-overbidding equilibrium μ∗ of the efficient tie-breaking FPA whose only non-trivial tie is at v. Among the players tying at v, there is only one player, 

i say, whose value exceeds v when he bids v, because if there were two or more such players, at least one of them could strictly increase his probability of winning by instead bidding slightly more than v. Hence, because only player i obtains positive utility from winning the tie, efficient tie-breaking ensures that the tie at v is broken in favor of player 

i. Since there are no other non-trivial ties, it is then straightforward to show that μ∗ is an equilibrium of the standard ( i v )-favoring FPA. Like Theorem 2, one can interpret Theorem 4 as another formal way of saying that ties at v are the only obstacle to equilibrium existence. One can also interpret Theorem 4 as suggesting a minimal change to the auction rules that guarantees equilibrium existence, provided that the auctioneer can identify an appropriate favored player i. In principle, the auctioneer can identify a favored player i if he knows the joint distribution of the players’ values, 24 but that can be a rather heavy informational burden as well as a difficult computational task. 25 Fortunately, there are interesting cases in which a favored player is easy to identify with a modest amount of information. Recall that, under regularity, there is always at least one player whose value is at least v. Let us assume just slightly more, namely, that there is a player whose value always exceeds v. The following result states that it is enough that the auctioneer can identify any such player. 24 The auctioneer can then, at least in principle, compute an equilibrium μ∗ of the efficient tie-breaking FPA (Theorem 1). If μ∗ has no non-trivial tie at v, then it is an equilibrium of the standard FPA and any player can be chosen as the favored player. If μ∗ has a non-trivial tie at v, then, as just argued, there is precisely one player who bids v when his value exceeds v, and this player can be chosen as the favored player. 25 Other FPA modifications that yield equilibrium existence include allowing players to submit payoff-irrelevant messages together with their bids (Lebrun (1996)) or employing a Vickrey tie-breaking rule (Maskin and Riley (2000)). While both of these modifications complicate tie-breaking by making it endogenous to the players’ behavior, they have the advantage of being implementable without any further requirements. EQUILIBRIUM EXISTENCE IN PRIVATE-VALUE FIRST-PRICE AUCTIONS 203 THEOREM 5: Suppose that ρ is regular . If player i’s value always exceeds v, then the (i v )-favoring FPA has a no-overbidding equilibrium .The proof of Theorem 5 is simple and we give it here. Suppose that player i’s value always exceeds v. By Theorems 1 and 2, either the standard FPA has an equilibrium, in which case any player, including i, can be chosen as the favored player, or there is a no-overbidding equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA in which v is the unique non-trivial tie as well as the lowest bid in the high-bid distribution. In the latter case, because 

i’s value always exceeds v, player i never bids less than v, because any such bid gives a payoff of zero while bidding slightly more than v gives him a strictly positive payoff. But then player i must bid v with positive probability (otherwise there would be no tie at v), and so player i must be the unique player whose value exceeds v when there is a tie at v.Hence, breaking ties in favor of i at v coincides with efficient tie-breaking and therefore preserves equilibrium incentives. Since there are no other non-trivial ties, we are done. 4.1. Revisiting Example 1 

It is instructive to discuss our results in the context of Example 1. If player 2’s value is known to be 2, then v = 2. Both players bidding 1 is then an equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA, and player 1 always wins the (only) non-trivial tie at 1. But this is not an equilibrium of the standard FPA. However, it can be modified to become an equilibrium by having player 1, who is indifferent between winning and losing the tie at 1, bid in an in-terval below 1 (thereby “losing the tie”) instead of bidding 1. In this particular case, many such modifications work. For example, player 1 can bid uniformly in any non-degenerate interval of the form [ x 1]. Notice that the non-trivial tie is at a bid that is strictly lower than the mHV, so the exceptional case identified in Theorem 2 is avoided, ensuring that our modification works. Indeed, condition (b) of Theorem 3 holds here, which itself im-plies that the exceptional case does not occur. If player 2’s value is equally likely to be 1 or 2, or is drawn uniformly from [1  2], then 

v = 1, none of the conditions of Theorem 3 hold, and the standard FPA has no equilib-rium. 26 Both players bidding 1 is an equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA, but this strategy profile cannot be modified at the non-trivial tie at 1, the mHV, to yield an equilib-rium. For example, having player 1 instead bid continuously in a non-degenerate interval of the form [ x 1] would make it profitable for player 2 to deviate to a bid below 1 when his value is 1 or close enough to 1. Indeed, the exceptional case in Theorem 2 occurs here because 0 < 1 = b = v.Let us turn now to Theorem 4. The only interesting cases to consider in Example 1 are those in which the standard FPA has no equilibrium, 27 that is, when player 2’s value is equally likely to be 1 or 2, or is drawn uniformly from [1  2]. Then v = 1, and both players bidding 1 with probability 1 is an equilibrium of the (2  v )-favoring FPA (but not the (1  v )-favoring FPA). Finally, notice that Theorem 5 applies when player 2’s value is drawn uniformly from [1  2] and implies that the (2  v )-favoring FPA has a no-overbidding equilibrium. 5. RESERVE PRICES AND SPECIAL SETTINGS We now consider FPAs with a reserve price and show that equilibrium existence con-tinues to turn on properties of the mHV in much the same way as without a reserve price. 26 See Proposition C.1 in Supplemental Appendix Section C. 27 Since any equilibrium of the standard FPA is an equilibrium of the ( i v )-favoring FPA for any player i.204 W. OLSZEWSKI, P. J. RENY, AND R. SIEGEL We then consider two special settings. In the first setting, each player has a symmetric counterpart. This happens, for example, if all players are symmetric. In the second set-ting, the players’ values are independent. We use Theorems 1 and 2 to establish existence results for these settings that are not covered by Theorem 3. 5.1. Reserve Prices 

A standard FPA with reserve price r ≥ 0 is the same as a standard FPA except that the highest bid weakly greater than r wins, and ties among high-bidders are again resolved by a fair lottery. If all players bid less than r (which includes bidding the non-participation bid −1), then the good is not sold and no payments are made. Theorem 3 deals with a reserve price of zero. The following result deals with positive reserve prices. THEOREM 6: Suppose that ρ is regular . Then the standard FPA with reserve price r > 0

has a no-overbidding equilibrium if the mHV , v, satisfies any of the following conditions :(a) v ≤ r.(b) Pr( ˜vi ≥ v) = 1 for exactly one player i.(c) Pr( ˜vi > v ) = 1 for at least two players i.(d) Pr( ˜vi ≤ v) = 1 for every player i.Condition (a) in Theorem 6 says that for any value higher than the reserve price, there is positive probability that the highest of the players’ values is less than that value. 28 Con-ditions (b)–(d) are the same as in Theorem 3. 5.2. Symmetric Players 

Theorem 1 shows that the efficient tie-breaking FPA always has an equilibrium, and the only reason that it might not be an equilibrium of the standard FPA is that it might involve non-trivial ties. The key observation of the present section is that if two players use the same strategy, then, for any bid b that wins with positive probability, there is probability zero that they bid b with a value not equal to b. Otherwise, by regularity, there would be a non-trivial tie at b that they would prefer to win or lose, contradicting equilibrium. Consequently, if each player’s strategy in an equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA is also used by at least one other player, then the only possible ties are trivial and the strategy profile must be an equilibrium of the standard FPA. So when each player has a symmetric counterpart, the standard FPA will have an equilibrium if the efficient tie-breaking FPA has an equilibrium in which symmetric players use the same strategy. RELATED LITERATURE In a seminal contribution, Lebrun (1996) introduced the efficient tie-breaking FPA— whose tie-breaking rule is not implementable—as a tool for proving equilibrium exis-tence in the standard FPA. Lebrun assumes that players have independent private values and quasi-linear utilities and shows that if the supports of their value-distributions are compact, have the same lower bound, and any value-atoms are not at that lower bound, then the standard FPA has an equilibrium. 31 He begins by appropriately discretizing the players’ value and bid spaces, and then takes limits of Nash equilibria of the discretized 30 The relevant statistic of the joint cost distribution is ¯c, the maximum low-cost, which is the highest value in the support of the distribution of min{ ˜c1     ˜cn}. When ρ is regular, ¯c = min{ ¯c1     ¯cn}, where ¯ci is the highest value in the support of ˜ci . Conditions (a)–(d) in Theorem 3 as well as the conditions in Theorem 8 can easily be formulated in terms of ¯c (instead of v). 31 Lebrun (1996) assumes that participation is mandatory. This complicates matters because ties at a bid of zero can then cause non-existence when precisely one of two, say, tying bidders has a positive value. If participation were not mandatory, the tying bidder with value zero could instead not participate. EQUILIBRIUM EXISTENCE IN PRIVATE-VALUE FIRST-PRICE AUCTIONS 207 games as the discretizations become arbitrarily fine. Assuming only that the players’ value-distributions are independent and have compact supports, Lebrun shows that the limit is an equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA. He then shows that this is also an equilibrium of the standard FPA unless there is a tie at the lowest bid in the support of the high-bid distribution. Finally, he shows that if the lowest value in the support of each player’s value-distribution is the same and no player has a value-atom there, then the high-bid distribution can have no atoms. Hence, under his assumptions, an equilib-rium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA exists and is also an equilibrium of the standard FPA. 32 Maskin and Riley (2000) use Lebrun’s (1996) technique, extending it to the case of interdependent values and non quasi-linear utilities. They are primarily interested in the existence of non-decreasing pure strategy equilibria and add additional assumptions for that purpose. Maskin and Riley’s (2000) results, when specialized to our private value setting, imply that the standard FPA has a non-decreasing equilibrium if each player’s utility is strictly increasing in his value, the joint distribution of theplayers’ values admits a density (which implies that all the marginals are atomless), and (i) the players’ utilities are weakly supermodular and their values are independently distributed or (ii) their utilities are log-supermodular and their values are affiliated. Although Maskin and Riley (2000) do not provide any results on the existence of an equilibrium in the standard FPA when the players’ value-distributions have atoms, their results imply (see the Corollary to their Proposition 2) that if the players have private values and each player has finitely many values, then an FPA with a Vickrey tie-breaking rule—where tying players participate in a Vickrey (1961) auction—has an equilibrium. An interesting consequence of our results is that Vickrey tie-breaking is in fact not necessary under rather mild conditions. Indeed, our Corollary 2 shows that if each player’s set of possible private values is finite and includes zero, and if all combinations of the players’ values have positive probability, then the standard FPA has an equilibrium. Jackson, Simon, Swinkels, and Zame (2002) and Jackson and Swinkels (2005) use an approach closely related to Lebrun’s (1996). They assume that utilities are quasi-linear, values are private, the joint distribution of values is absolutely continuous with respect to the product of its marginals, and each player’s value-distribution is atomless with compact support. Under these assumptions, they show that there always exists some, possibly non-implementable, tie-breaking rule under which an FPA has an equilibrium. The assump-tion that each player’s value-distribution is atomless ensures that any equilibrium with any non-standard tie-breaking rule is also an equilibrium with the standard tie-breaking. Equilibrium existence results for the case of independent values have also been ob-tained by studying the system of differential equations generated by the players’ first-order necessary conditions for utility maximization. Lebrun (1999) takes such an ap-proach. Assuming independent private values and quasi-linear utilities, he shows that the standard FPA with reserve price r > 0 has an equilibrium in non-decreasing pure strate-gies if the supports of the players’ value-distributions are all the same interval whose lower endpoint is r and either every player’s value-distribution is atomless or every player’s value-distribution has exactly one atom, and that atom is at r. In the latter case, Lebrun also shows that the equilibrium strategies are essentially unique. Thus, Lebrun (1999) al-lows exactly one atom in each player’s value-distribution at precisely the point where no atom is allowed by Lebrun (1996). 32 Lebrun (1996) also permits a reserve price that is weakly lower than each player’s lowest possible value. 208 W. OLSZEWSKI, P. J. RENY, AND R. SIEGEL The reserve price, r, plays an important role in Lebrun’s (1999) existence result, where it is assumed that r is the lower endpoint of an interval that is the common support of the players’ value-distributions. When that lower endpoint r is positive and is an atom of each player’s value-distribution, the essentially unique equilibrium whose existence Lebrun (1999) establishes may require all but one of the players not to participate— or to bid less than r—when their value is r so that the remaining player can win with probability 1 when he bids r and his value is between r and some value v > r . Without the reserve price, this construction does not work because the remaining player, when his value is close to r, would then strictly prefer to bid less than r. Consequently, the other players, when their values are r, would want to bid high enough, but less than r, to win with positive probability and the remaining player would no longer win with probability 1 when his value is between v and r.Relatedly, the case in which the players’ independent value-distributions have strictly positive densities on a common interval and have a single atom at the strictly positive lower endpoint is not covered by Lebrun (1996, 1999) or by our Theorem 3. However, our Theorem 8 in Section 5 covers this case and additional cases. The first existence re-sult that we are aware of for any such case is due to Kaplan and Zamir (2012). They study the special case of two players with quasi-linear utilities whose independent private values are drawn uniformly from compact intervals. They solve the associated pair of differen-tial equations and provide closed form expressions for the players’ equilibrium bidding strategies in the standard FPA with a reserve price. When the reserve price is in the inte-rior of the players’ value-supports, they note that, because the strategies they obtain are strictly increasing above the reserve price, these strategies also happen to form an equi-librium of the standard FPA without a reserve price when the players’ value-distributions have an atom at their common lower endpoint and are uniform on the remainder of their intervals. 33 8. CONCLUSION This paper identifies the mHV—the minimum value in the support of the high-value distribution—as a key statistic of the joint distribution of values that often determines whether or not an equilibrium exists in the standard FPA with private values. Without assuming independent values, quasi-linear utilities, or atomless value-distributions, we use this statistic to derive permissive sufficient conditions for equilibrium existence, and we also show that even when these conditions fail, existence can be ensured by modifying the standard tie-breaking rule at just one bid, the mHV, and in a way that does not require knowledge of any player’s private information. We also derive sufficient conditions for equilibrium existence when the players’ value-distributions exhibit some symmetry or are independent, as well as when the auction includes a reserve price. From a methodological perspective, we extend the reach of Lebrun’s (1996) and Maskin and Riley’s (2000) result on the existence of an equilibrium in the efficient tie-breaking FPA by showing that, for the case of private values, this auction satisfies the hypothe-ses of Reny’s (1999) equilibrium existence theorem for discontinuous games even when the only assumption on the joint distribution of values is that it is absolutely continuous with respect to the product of its marginals. In particular, for the efficient tie-breaking 33 Kaplan and Zamir (2012) also obtain positive existence results when the players’ value-supports are inter-vals with distinct lower endpoints precisely one of which is an atom. These results are covered by condition (b) of our Theorem 3. EQUILIBRIUM EXISTENCE IN PRIVATE-VALUE FIRST-PRICE AUCTIONS 209 FPA, we make no assumptions on the supports of the players’ value-distributions except non-negativity, accommodating unbounded supports and arbitrary atoms, and we allow the players’ utilities to be non quasi-linear, unbounded, and non-monotone in their bids. Allowing non-monotonicity permits us to apply our results to an incomplete information model of Bertrand price competition when each firm’s constant marginal cost is its private information. Together, our Theorems 3, 6, 7, and 8 strictly generalize all of the equilibrium existence results in the literature that we are aware of for the standard FPA with private values with or without a reserve price. 34 Even so, the sufficient conditions for equilibrium existence in Theorems 3, 7, and 8 are not necessary for equilibrium existence in the standard FPA. Completely closing the gap between sufficiency and necessity seems difficult. In Section B of Supplemental Appendix Olszewski, Reny and Siegel (2026), we explore the size of the gap in the case of two bidders with quasi-linear utility and independent values. In Section C of Supplemental Appendix Olszewski, Reny and Siegel (2026), we provide some non-existence results to illustrate directions in which our results cannot be extended. 9. PROOFS We begin by stating two lemmas whose proofs are given in Section A of Supplemental Appendix Olszewski, Reny and Siegel (2026). In the main text, we introduced two non-standard tie-breaking rules—efficient tie-breaking and ( i v )-favorable tie-breaking. The first lemma applies to FPAs with these tie-breaking rules as well as to any FPA, imple-mentable or not, in which ties among high-bidders are resolved according to a lottery that depends (measurably) on the vector of bids ( b1     b n) and the vector of realized values (v1     v n). When, in the statement of Lemma 1, we say “some,” “every,” or “all” FPAs, we are referring to FPAs in this class. Note that a player’s set of strategies is unchanged across all FPAs because distinct FPAs differ only in their tie-breaking rules. LEMMA 1: Let μ be a strategy profile for some FPA . Then , ( i) for each player i, the supre-mum of i’s payoff (over all of his strategies ) against μ−i is the same across all FPAs , ( ii ) if 

μ is an equilibrium of some FPA and no player’s payoff changes when μ is played in some other FPA , then μ is also an equilibrium of the other FPA , ( iii ) if μ is an equilibrium of some FPA and every tie of μ is trivial , then μ is an equilibrium of every FPA , and (iv ) if μ is an equilibrium of the standard FPA , then every tie of μ is trivial .The next lemma states that, under regularity, joint events that are products of individual events have positive probability if and only if each of the individual events has positive probability. This accounts for why many of the arguments that hold under independence also hold under regularity. 34 Our Theorem 3 parts (b) and (c) generalizes Maskin and Riley (2000), Jackson, Simon, Swinkels, and Zame (2002), and Jackson and Swinkels (2005). The final sentence and part (c) of our Theorem 3 generalizes Lebrun (1996) when the reserve price is zero, and the final sentence and part (c) of our Theorem 6 generalizes Lebrun (1996) when the reserve price is positive. Our Theorem 6 part (a) and our Theorem 8 each generalize Lebrun (1999), and our Theorem 8 generalizes all of the existence results in Kaplan and Zamir (2012) when values and reserve prices are non-negative. We emphasize that, for these papers, we generalize only their results on the existence of an equilibrium of the standard FPA (with and without a reserve price) with a single good and private values. Most of these papers have objectives and results that go beyond our more narrow focus on equilibrium existence. 210 W. OLSZEWSKI, P. J. RENY, AND R. SIEGEL LEMMA 2: Suppose that ρ is regular . Then , for any measurable subsets E1     E n of 

[0  ∞), Pr( ˜v1 ∈ E1     ˜vn ∈ En) > 0 iff Pr( ˜vi ∈ Ei) > 0 for every i = 1     n .Before proving Theorem 1, we state and prove a key lemma. LEMMA 3: Suppose that μ∗ is a full-participation , no-overbidding equilibrium of the ef-ficient tie-breaking FPA and b is a tie of μ∗. Then , ( i) some player’s value-distribution has an atom at b. If , in addition , ρ is regular , then (ii ) b is the lowest value in the support of the high-bid distribution of μ∗ and (iii ) when the tie at b is non-trivial , there is precisely one player for whom there is positive probability that his bid is b and his value strictly exceeds b.P ROOF OF LEMMA 3: We first prove (i). Since b is a tie and there is no overbidding, there is positive probability of the event E that two or more players submit the high bid of 

b and each of their values is at least b. If in this event there is positive probability that two or more of these players’ values strictly exceed b, then because at least one of them will lose the tie with positive probability, that player can strictly increase his payoff by bidding slightly more than b instead of b, contradicting equilibrium. Hence, in the event E, there is probability zero that two or more of these players’ values strictly exceed b so there is positive probability that at least one of their values is equal to b. Hence, b is an atom of one of their value-distributions. To prove (ii), let b be the lowest bid in the support of the high-bid distribution of μ∗.Then b ≥ 0 by full participation. We must show that, under regularity, b = b. Since the high-bid distribution under μ∗ has an atom at b, we have b ≤ b. By the proof of (i), there is a player, k say, whose value-distribution has an atom at b and who bids b with positive probability when his value is b. Suppose, by way of contradiction, that b < b . For i̸ = k,let E i be the set of i’s values at which i bids less than b with positive probability. Then Pr( ˜vi ∈ Ei) > 0 for every i̸ = k because there is positive probability that all players simul-taneously, let alone separately, bid less than b (since b < b ). Since b is an atom of player 

k’s value-distribution, if we let Ek = {b}, then Pr( ˜vi ∈ Ei) > 0 for each player i. Since ρ is regular, Lemma 2 implies that Pr( ˜vi ∈ Ei for every player i) > 0. Hence, there is positive probability that player k’s value is b and all the other players bid less than b. So player k

bidding b with positive probability in the positive probability event in which his value is b

is not a best reply (because in that event he could get a positive payoff if he instead bid slightly less than b), contradicting that μ∗ is an equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA. Hence, b = b.We now prove (iii). Since b is a non-trivial tie and no player bids above his value (no overbidding), there is, for at least one player, positive probability that that player bids b

and his value strictly exceeds b. To prove (iii), we must show that, under regularity, there is at most one such player. Suppose there are two such players, 1 and 2 say. Let E1 be the set of values for player 1 that strictly exceed b and that bid b with positive probability, and define E2 similarly for player 2. By our supposition, Pr( ˜v1 ∈ E1 ) > 0 and Pr( ˜v2 ∈ E2 ) > 0. For i > 2, let Ei be the set of values for player i that bid at most b with positive probability. Then Pr( ˜vi ∈ Ei) > 0 for each i > 2 because there is positive probability that the highest bid is b. Since ρ is regular, Lemma 2 implies that Pr( ˜vi ∈ Ei for every player i) > 0. Hence, there is positive probability that the highest bid is b and players 1 and 2 both bid b and have values that strictly exceed b. Then, at least one of them can strictly increase his payoff by bidding slightly more than b instead of bidding b when μ∗ calls upon him to do so. But this contradicts equilibrium and proves (iii). Q.E.D. EQUILIBRIUM EXISTENCE IN PRIVATE-VALUE FIRST-PRICE AUCTIONS 211 P ROOF OF THEOREM 1: Consider the efficient tie-breaking FPA, but restrict each player to strategies that never bid −1 and that never bid more than their value. That is, each player i is restricted to the subset Si of distributional strategies that assign prob-ability 1 to the closed set of value/bid pairs {( vi  b i) : 0 ≤ bi ≤ vi < ∞}. The set of strate-gies Si is a nonempty, convex, and weak*-closed subset of the set of probability mea-sures on ℝ2

> +

.35 Moreover, it is a tight set of probability measures and hence, being closed, is weak*-compact by Prohorov’s theorem (see Billingsley (1968)). 36 We claim that the resulting strategy-restricted efficient tie-breaking FPA is payoff secure and reciprocally upper-semicontinuous. 37 It is payoff secure because, given a strategy profile, the payoff of any player i can be secured by changing i’s strategy so that whenever it specifies a bid that is less than i’s value, player i instead submits a slightly higher bid that is less than his value. For reciprocal upper-semicontinuity, it suffices to show that the sum of the players’ payoffs is upper-semicontinuous in the weak* topology as a function of their strategies. We now show this upper-semicontinuity. Under the efficient tie-breaking rule, for any non-negative vector of values v =

(v1     v n) and for any non-negative vector of bids b = (b1     b n), the sum of the play-ers’ (ex post) utilities is

U n)f . Hence, by Lemma A.7 of Kadan, Reny, and Swinkels (2017), the sum of the players’ (ex ante expected) payoffs is upper-semicontinuous in the weak* topology as a function of their strategies. The strategy-restricted efficient tie-breaking FPA is therefore reciprocally upper-semicontinuous. Since the strategy-restricted efficient tie-breaking FPA is payoff secure and reciprocally upper-semicontinuous, Corollary 3.3 in Reny (1999) implies that it has a Nash equilib-rium, μ∗ say, when the set of strategies is restricted as above. Moreover, in the efficient tie-breaking FPA in which the players’ strategies are not restricted, μ∗ clearly exhibits full participation and no overbidding, and is also an equilibrium there because bidding 

−1 (i.e., choosing to not participate) or bidding above one’s value are never better than bidding one’s value, and the option to bid one’s value, whatever it may be, is always avail-able with the restricted strategy sets. This proves that the efficient tie-breaking FPA has a no-overbidding equilibrium with full participation. That a tie can occur only at an atom of some player’s value-distribution follows from part (i) of Lemma 3, and that μ∗ is an equilibrium of the standard FPA if it has no non-trivial ties follows from part (iii) of Lemma 1. 212 W. OLSZEWSKI, P. J. RENY, AND R. SIEGEL We now state two additional lemmas, followed by the proofs of the remaining theorems. The proofs of the lemmas are in Supplemental Appendix Section A. LEMMA 4: Let μ∗ be an equilibrium of any FPA , and let b be the lowest bid in the support of the high-bid distribution of μ∗. If ρ is regular , then , for each player i, there is probability zero that player i bids less than b and his value exceeds b.LEMMA 5: Let μ be any no-overbidding strategy profile , and let b be the lowest bid in the support of the high-bid distribution of μ. If v is the mHV , then b ≤ v.P ROOF OF THEOREM 2: Suppose that μ∗ is a full-participation, no-overbidding equi-librium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA that is not an equilibrium of the standard FPA. Letting b denote the lowest bid in the support of the high-bid distribution, we have 0 ≤ b

by full participation and b ≤ v by Lemma 5. Hence, 0 ≤ b ≤ v. Moreover, by Lemma 4, under μ∗ no player bids less than b when his value exceeds b. Finally, part (iii) of Lemma 1 and part (ii) of Lemma 3 together imply that μ∗ has precisely one tie, and that tie is non-trivial and occurs at b. For the remainder of the proof, we fix μ∗ and b as obtained above, and all random variables and event probabilities are those induced by μ∗, unless stated otherwise. Our remaining task is to show that unless 0 < b = v holds, μ∗ can be modified to be-come a no-overbidding equilibrium of the standard FPA and this modification exhibits full participation if 0 < b < v .We observe that the reason μ∗ is not an equilibrium of the standard FPA (even though 

μ∗ is an equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA) is that the non-trivial tie at b is re-solved by a fair lottery. Consequently, by part (iii) of Lemma 3, there is precisely one tying player who obtains positive utility from winning the tie and this player is strictly better off deviating from b to a slightly higher bid. To remove such profitable deviations when b > 0, we will modify μ∗ so that whenever a player is supposed to bid b when his value is b, he instead bids randomly in a small interval below b. The distribution of modified bids below 

b must be chosen carefully so as not to create new profitable deviations. When b = 0, we will modify μ∗ so that bidders choose not to participate instead of bidding zero when their values are zero. Since, by hypothesis, 0 < b = v does not hold, and since 0 ≤ b ≤ v, there are two cases to consider, namely, b = 0 and 0 < b < v .Suppose first that b = 0. By part (iii) of Lemma 3, there is probability zero that two or more players simultaneously bid b and their values exceed b. Consequently, when two or more players tie at b, the value of at most one of them exceeds b and, by no overbidding, all the other tying players have value b and are indifferent between winning and losing the tie. Then, by efficient tie-breaking, the tie is broken in favor of the only player who strictly prefers to win the tie. Consequently, we can modify μ∗ so that whenever any bidder has value b = 0, instead of bidding zero (his only non-negative bid that is not above his value) he bids −1 (i.e., chooses not to participate). With this modification, which affects no bidder’s payoff in the efficient tie-breaking FPA, the modified strategy profile remains a no-overbidding equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA. But now there are no non-trivial ties, so by part (iii) of Lemma 1 the modified strategy profile is a no-overbidding equilibrium of the standard FPA. Next, suppose that 0 < b < v . We will again modify μ∗ to eliminate the non-trivial tie at

Consequently, whenever player i’s value is in [ v v + δ], he is strictly better off choosing some bid strictly below v = b and winning with positive probability, than bidding b or higher. Hence, because there is positive probability that i’s value is in [v v + δ] and we are in equilibrium, player i must win with a bid below b with positive probability. But this contradicts the definition of b, so (b) cannot hold. Suppose that (c) holds. Then Pr( ˜vi > v ) = 1 for at least two players i, say players 1 and 2. Hence, because b = v, we have Pr( ˜vi > b ) = 1 for i = 1 2. Since players 1 and 2 bid at least b whenever their values exceed b (Lemma 4), they both always bid at least b. Hence, because b is a tie of μ∗, players 1 and 2 must simultaneously bid b with positive probability. But because their values always exceed b, this contradicts part (iii) of Lemma 3. Hence, (c) cannot hold. Finally, suppose that (d) holds. Since b is a non-trivial tie of μ∗ and no player overbids, there is positive probability that the highest bid is b and at least two players bid b, each of their values is at least b, and at least one of their values exceeds b. Since b = v, this means that there is positive probability that some player’s value exceeds v, which directly contradicts (d). Hence, (d) cannot hold. Since, by the hypotheses of Theorem 3, at least one of (a)–(d) holds, we may conclude from what we have just shown that 0 < b = v does not hold. Therefore, because we are supposing that μ∗ is not an equilibrium of the standard FPA, Theorem 2 implies that μ∗

can be modified to become a no-overbidding equilibrium of the standard FPA, proving the first part of Theorem 3. To complete the proof, suppose, in addition, that the value-distributions of at least two players, players 1 and 2 say, have no atom at zero. Since EQUILIBRIUM EXISTENCE IN PRIVATE-VALUE FIRST-PRICE AUCTIONS 219 0 < b = v does not hold and 0 ≤ b ≤ v, either b = 0 or 0 < b < v . If b = 0, then because b is a tie of μ∗ and μ∗ exhibits full participation, there is positive probability of the event, let us call it E, that all the players simultaneously bid b = 0. But because the value-distributions of neither player 1 nor player 2 have an atom at 0, the values of players 1 and 2 almost surely exceed 0 in the event E, contradicting part (iii) of Lemma 3. Therefore, 0 < b < v, so by Theorem 2, μ∗ can be modified to become a full-participation, no-overbidding equilibrium of the standard FPA. Q.E.D. 

PROOF OF THEOREM 4: We may assume that the standard FPA does not have a no-overbidding equilibrium since otherwise, by parts (iii) and (iv) of Lemma 1, any such equilibrium would be an equilibrium of every FPA, including every ( i v )-favoring FPA for any player i and we would be done. Let μ∗ be a no-overbidding equilibrium of the efficient tie-breaking FPA, whose existence follows from Theorem 1, and let b be the lowest bid in the support of the high-bid distribution under μ∗. Then, by Theorem 2, b is the only tie of μ∗, it is a non-trivial tie, and 0 < b = v.By part (iii) of Lemma 3, there is precisely one player, player i say, who, with positive probability, ties with a high bid of b = v and would receive positive utility from winning the tie. Hence, under efficient tie-breaking, player i wins all ties at b that he is involved in unless all of the tying players, including i, are indifferent between winning and losing the tie. Consequently, because b = v is the only tie of μ∗, no player’s payoff would change under μ∗ if the auction rules were switched from those of the efficient tie-breaking FPA to those of the standard ( i v )-favoring FPA. Consequently, by part (ii) of Lemma 1, μ∗ is an equilibrium of the standard ( i v )-favoring FPA. Q.E.D.

facts: In first-price auctions, asymmetries among bidders typically result in inefficient allocations, meaning the winner may not be the person who values the object the most.

First-price auctions with resale do have a unique monotonic equilibrium under independent private values, suggesting that adding post-auction trading institutions can restore equilibrium uniqueness even with asymmetric bidders.
https://www.mdc.e.u-tokyo.ac.jp/wp/wp-co.url_content:
Asymmetric Auctions with Resale



> 87

In a first-price auction, asymmetries among bidders typically result in inefficient allocations— that is, the winner of the auction may not be the person who values the object the most. This inefficiency creates a motive for post -auction resale, and when bidders take resale possibilities into account, their bidding behavior is affected as well. Standard models of such auctions, by and large, implicitly assume either that resale possibilities do not exist or that bidders do not take these into account when formulating bids. There are at least two reasons why resale possibilities should be considered explicitly. The first one is positive. If, after the auction is over, bidders see that there are potential gains from trade, then they will naturally engage in such trade. And it seems unlikely that the seller can prevent bidders from engaging in post-auction trade, even if, for some reason, resale was deemed dis-advantageous. In the auction of spectrum licenses in the United Kingdom in 2000, post-auction trade was restricted by the government. The bidders, however, were easily able to circumvent these restrictions. TIW, a Canadian firm, bid successfully for the most valuable license “A” with a winning bid in excess of £4 billion. Hutchison, a telecommunications company, then acquired the license by buying TIW itself.  Similarly, after the auction, France Telecom, an unsuccessful bidder, acquired Orange, a successful bidder. British Telecom created a wholly owned subsidiary that bid successfully in the auction. After the auction, this subsidiary was floated on the stock market and sold. Thus, restrictions on the buying and selling of licenses were circumvented by the buying and selling of companies that owned the licenses. The actions of Hutchison and British Telecom prior to the auction suggest that bidders fully anticipated post-auction resale possibilities.  Actually, Hutchison had acquired a small stake in TIW prior to the auction and, in fact, provided funds for its bid. We are grateful to Kenneth Binmore and Tilman Börgers for providing us with details of the UK spectrum auctions. 

# Asymmetric Auctions with Resale 

By Isa Hafalir and Vijay Krishna* 

We study first- and second-price auctions with resale in a model with indepen-dent private values. With asymmetric bidders, the resulting inefficiencies create a motive for post-auction trade which, in our model, takes place via monopoly pricing—the winner makes a take-it-or-leave-it offer to the loser. We show (a) a first-price auction with resale has a unique monotonic equilibrium; and (b) with resale, the expected revenue from a first-price auction exceeds that from a second-price auction. The inclusion of resale possibilities thus permits a gen-eral revenue ranking of the two auctions that is not available when these are excluded. The second reason to consider auctions with resale is normative. There has been recent inter-est in the design of efficient auctions, especially in the context of privatization. If post-auction resale results in efficiency, however, then from the planner’s perspective, an inefficient auction is just as good. Are the allocations from an inefficient auction followed by post-auction resale indeed efficient? In this paper, we study the effects of post-auction resale in a simple model with two bidders whose private values are independently, but perhaps asymmetrically, distributed. Equilibrium allocations in first-price auctions are then inefficient and bidders have the incentive to engage in post-auction trade. In our basic model, resale takes place via monopoly pricing—the winner of the auction makes a take-it-or-leave-it offer to the loser. We first show that every equilibrium has the feature that, despite the asymmetries, the distribu-tions of bids of the two bidders are identical (Proposition  ). Symmetry would not be surprising if resale took place under complete information and so were always efficient. In that case, each bidder would bid as if his value were the maximum of the two values. In our model, the symme-try of bid distributions is striking because post-auction resale also takes place under incomplete information and so is not always efficient. Here it occurs as a result of some cost-benefit calcula-tions at the margin. The symmetry of the bid distributions is key—we show in Theorem   how it may be used to construct an equilibrium of the first-price auction with resale. The possibility of resale also affects incentives in second-price auctions. It is no longer a dominant strategy to bid one’s value. It is, nevertheless, a robust equilibrium—the strategies do not depend on the value distributions (Proposition 2). In this equilibrium, of course, the auction allocates efficiently and so there is no resale. Our main result (Theorem 2) is that once resale possibilities are admitted, the expected reve-nue from a first-price auction exceeds that from a second-price auction. We thus obtain a general 

revenue ranking of the two auction formats. We require only that the value distributions be regu-lar in the sense defined by Roger Myerson ( 98 ), ensuring that the monopoly pricing problem at the resale stage is well behaved. We remind the reader that in the absence of resale, the two auctions cannot be unambiguously ranked (see the work of Eric Maskin and John Riley 2000). The proof of Theorem 2 uses a technique borrowed from the calculus of variations. To the best of our knowledge, the use of this technique is new to auction theory and will, perhaps, prove useful in other applications as well. The results reported above concern a particular resale institution—monopoly pricing—in which the winner of the auction has all the bargaining power. In Section V, we show, however, that this is inessential by considering more general resale mechanisms. In particular, we examine the monopsony mechanism in which the loser has all the bargaining power and then, more gen-erally, mechanisms in which bargaining power is shared, perhaps unequally. All of the results reported above extend to these alternative, and more general, resale institutions. 

Related Literature .—Equilibrium analysis of asymmetric first-price auctions has posed many challenges. Some of the difficulties were already pointed out by William Vickrey ( 96 ) in his pioneering paper. He constructed an example in which bidder  ’s value, say a, was commonly known while the other’s was uniformly distributed. In that case, there is an equilibrium of the first-price auction in which bidder   randomizes. Vickrey showed that for some values of a, the revenue from a first-price auction exceeded that from a second-price auction; for other values of 

a, the revenue ranking was reversed. Since then, progress in the area has been sporadic at best. In asymmetric first-price auctions without resale, pure strategy equilibria exist under quite permissive conditions, as a consequence of general existence results (see, for instance, Philip Reny  999, Susan Athey 200 , or Matthew Jackson and Jeroen Swinkels 2005). There is, again under weak conditions, a unique equilibrium VOL. 98 NO. 1 89 hA fALIR  AN d K RI sh NA : Asy MMETRI c Auc TION s WIT h R EsALE 

(see, for instance, the work of Maskin and Riley (2003) or that of Bernard Lebrun (2006)). But characterization results and revenue comparisons are few and far between. James H. Griesmer, Richard E. Levitan, and Martin Shubik ( 967) derive closed-form equilibrium bidding strategies in a first-price auction in which bidders draw values from uniform distributions, but over differ-ent supports. Michael Plum ( 992) extends this to situations in which the two value distributions are of the form v n

, again over different supports. Harrison Cheng (2006) identifies a class of distribution pairs which lead to linear bidding strategies. For this class, he shows that the first-price auction is revenue superior to the second-price auction. For specific examples of distribu-tion pairs, Estelle Cantillon (forthcoming) shows how asymmetry affects revenue in first-price auctions. In the absence of general analytic results, some researchers have resorted to numerical methods (see, for instance, the work of Robert Marshall et al.  994). Maskin and Riley (2000) derive the most comprehensive characterization and revenue ranking results concerning first- and second-price auctions in the presence of asymmetries. They con-sider problems in which one of the bidders is unambiguously stronger than the other. Specifically, the distribution of one bidder (conditionally) stochastically dominates that of the other. Maskin and Riley (2000) are able to identify circumstances in which one or the other auction is revenue superior. For instance, the second-price auction is revenue superior if the distribution of the weak bidder is obtained from that of the strong bidder by reassigning probability mass toward lower values. Gadi Fibich, Arieh Gavious, and Aner Sela (2004) have shown that when the bidders are “nearly symmetric,” the difference in revenues is of a smaller magnitude than the difference, appropriately measured, in the underlying distributions. Thus, for small asymmetries, the auc-tions are nearly revenue equivalent. Madhurima Gupta and Lebrun ( 999) consider resale possibilities in a manner not unlike this paper. They assume, however, that at the end of the auction both values are announced. This means, of course, that resale is always efficient. But it is not clear how the auctioneer would come to know the values themselves. In contrast, in our model, the auctioneer knows only the bids and not the values. Philip Haile (2003) considers resale possibilities in a symmetric model. At the time of bidding, however, buyers have only noisy information regarding their true values, which are revealed to them only after the auction. There is a motive for resale because although the win-ner of the auction may have received the highest signal, he may not have the highest true value. No general revenue ranking obtains. Charles Zheng (2002) identifies conditions under which the outcomes of Myerson’s ( 98 ) optimal auction can be achieved when resale is permitted. The model of Rod Garratt and Thomas Tröger (2006) is closest to ours in spirit. The crucial difference is that they assume, as in Vickrey’s ( 96 ) example, that the value of one of the bidders is commonly known and, moreover, is equal to zero. This bidder, thus, participates in the auction for purely “speculative” reasons—he has no use value for the object. He benefits only if he can resell the object to the other bidder. 2 In the efficient equilibrium of the second-price auction, the revenue is obviously zero. Garratt and Tröger (2006) show that there is a unique mixed strategy equilibrium in the first-price auction in which the revenue is positive. We allow for general con-tinuous distributions and so their model may be viewed as a limiting case of ours.

II. First-Price  Auction  with  Resale 

Our model of the first-price auction with resale (FPAR) is the following. The buyers first par-ticipate in a standard sealed-bid first-price auction. The winning bid is publicly announced. We assume—as is common in real-world auctions—that the losing bid is not announced. 5

In the second stage, the winner of the auction—say j—may, if he wishes, offer to sell the object to the other bidder i Z j at some price p. If the offer is accepted by i, a sale ensues. If the offer is rejected, the original owner j retains the object. Thus resale takes place via a take-it-or-leave-it offer by the winner of the auction. 6

Note that if i loses the auction, then the announcement of the winning bid bj carries no useful information—that is, whether i will accept an offer is independent of what he believes j’s value to be. Thus, the equilibrium would be unaffected if neither bid were announced. As usual, we work backward and first outline behavior in the resale stage. A. Resale  stage

In this section, we establish that the first-price auction with resale has a pure strategy equi-librium in which each bidder follows a strictly increasing bidding strategy. The equilibrium is unique in the class of pure strategy equilibria with nondecreasing bidding strategies. The proof that there is a strictly increasing equilibrium is constructive. Given regular distribu-tion functions  fs and  fw, construct  f as in (9). Consider a symmetric first-price auction in which each bidder draws values independently from  f.
